sentences.text: Yes.
So the Russian had come across Auschwitz now.
And that was the first man who came in, but behind him was-- they came on horses, with horse carriages.
And they brought their guns and everything.
And they were going to camp in the camp.
Of course, we couldn't really talk with them.
They couldn't talk with us because, you know, language problem.
But they realized who we were and we realized that they were fighting.
And then later-- and they never stayed long.
Stayed for a few hours or sometimes a day and then went on again, because this was the first fighting unit to approach.
And, but they were always followed with-- quite rapid another group came, another group came.
And some of the people did speak German then and Polish.
And this one Polish woman with us could speak with them.
So we heard some terrible stories from them, what had happened in the Polish villages, how the Germans had treated the Poles and the Russians.
And those people were the forefront and they were going into Germany to revenge themselves.
And they were-- some of the Russian army, there were young boys who were not-- were about 15, 16, who had been partisans and when the army came they joined up with them.
And had lost their whole village, all their family.
And they really went to kill and to revenge themselves.
So those stories of when the Russians entered German towns, that they were looted and raping and-- were true.

But it wasn't just because they were wicked, but because they had really lost everything they had and they were really full of such hate and revenge that they were going to take it out on the first Germans they met.
So they were very good to us.
They-- when they opened a field kitchen there, they cooked cabbage soup and was-- shared whatever they had.
It was very hard for us on the stomach because we had had only [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very little, basic food and suddenly we ate this heavy, cabbage soup.
And we ate whatever we got.
We never stopped eating.
And we never stopped going to the toilet.
We had no toilet, so we used a bucket.
And sometimes I ate practically the whole day and sat the whole night on the bucket with terrible cramps.
And, you know, I thought my whole inside was coming out.
Which was indeed dangerous because a lot of the older people, when they did get food, just died from-- the body just couldn't cope with it.
But, you know, luckily I could and we just ate.
And with bad results, but we still carried on eating.
Food was very, very important to us.
And so that went again on for several days.
And one day we went to where the Russian had just left.
We looked what they left behind.
And we saw there was a dead horse lying on the frozen ground.
And the Polish woman said, ah, I can make a wonderful horse stew out of that.
Go back and get a knife and we'll cut this horse open before it gets hard frozen.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a bit doubtful, but, nevertheless, I did that.
And went and got this big knife and started to cut into this horse.
And the tummy part is the softest.
And so we cut out a funny round thing and we carried it to the hut.
And she put the with the fire and she was going to cut it.
And when we cut it we saw that it was a womb and it was a little horse in it.
And the Russians had choked it because it was pregnant and they couldn't take it along.
So we still cut a bit of it and made a stew and ate it.
You know, when I think about that, that I did this, I just think that can't be true.
But I know it is true.
And, I mean, we did have food already, but, as I say, there was just-- we just had to eat all the time, whatever.
So after a time we said, look, we don't know what will happen.
It can go on for long time.
We have to really decide what we were going to do.
We're going to go and look for-- we knew Auschwitz was not very far, the main camp.
Let's go to Auschwitz and see if we can find the men, because we had-- the Russian had said they go on to Auschwitz.
And that night when we were sleeping, we heard a knock on the door and we didn't know who was coming.
But eventually we opened up.
And it was a-- a German, a Nazi, who was escaping, was running away from the Russians.
The Russians was there after him.
And he said first he is a soldier and, you know, he lost his battalion and so, and could we keep him there the night?
And give him some food?
He has been running.
And we didn't dare to say no, because, you know, he was armed and we didn't want him there either.
So this Polish woman said, oh, I wouldn't stay here.
The Russians have just been and they said they are coming back.
So, you know, it's too dangerous for you to stay here.
So he thought it was good enough in.
So he said, which way shall-- do we think shall I go?
And she sent him the direction of into the Russians.
So later on when we-- we went to Auschwitz.
But we came back and later on the Russians caught him, we saw that, and he was an SS.
Because all the SS was tattooed.
Not [INAUDIBLE] only tattoo us.
They tattooed the SS under the arm with some kind of a-- I don't know if it was a SS sign or number or something, but they were all marked.
So they did shoot him.
We saw that, that they shot this.
But talking about-- so he didn't escape.
In the camp when-- sometimes people did try to escape, but it wasn't because they wanted to escape or thought they can escape.
But it was really more a sign that they wanted to die and gave up.
Because none of the Jewish inmates ever did escape.
Occasionally a Pole did escape because they were sometimes even from the area and they knew people outside and they could speak the language and they might have known where to go.
So occasionally a Pole escaped.
But from the Jews, never anybody escape.
There were-- or when you went on a work commando and you walked outside, sometimes they just ran out of line.
And there were several things which happened to them.
Or they were shot because all their work commandos were walking with armed soldiers and soldiers with their dogs.
So sometimes they were shot on the spot.
Sometimes a dog was let loose and the dog just tore the person apart.
Sometimes the person was brought back into the camp and then we had to-- the whole camp had to stand on the center square.
And they put up a gallow and we all had to watch and see when the person was hanged.
Of course-- You saw this?
We saw it happening.
But, of course, nobody could force us to look.
So, you know, we closed our eyes.
We just couldn't-- but we had to stand there for hours to see this happening.
That happened several times.
So sometimes a person, if he had given up, threw himself onto the electrified wire and just burned.
That we saw as well occasionally.
You saw that.
Yes.
But, actually, you know, very few people tried to escape because as long as there is life, there is hope.
So we knew-- everybody knew if you escape, it's death definitely.
And like this, there is a little chance that you come through.
So not many people did it.
So, coming back to the freedom.
Of course we were tremendously relieved, but, you know, we were not yet convinced that we'll survive, that we had really come through.
Because we were still loose, you know?
And, like, the Germans had come back once and the Russians themself had mentioned, when they could speak Polish or German, that they were in the middle of the front.
And it could be that they would lose the position and they might retreat again and the Germans might come back.
It was not at all a complete march forward yet.
It was a backwards and forwards and shooting and airplanes and everything.
So we were not yet completely out of the woods.
So we decided we would go to the men's camp where the Russian had said they had set up headquarters.
So the French girl and me-- we were the youngest and the strongest-- decided we would go and come back and report and then decide if we would go away or not.
And so it was-- everything was covered in snow and there were no roads.
And we didn't really know how far it is or anything.
But, again, how we did it, we don't know.
We went and we got there.
And that was a lot of activity there.
There were lots of Russians there.
There were quite a lot, several hundred of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] liberated prisoners.
And there were-- Auschwitz men camp, as I said to you, was buildings where the officers were.
And there were two-story buildings with proper toilets, proper water-- it didn't work at that time.
It was partly frozen and so on.
But it was still proper buildings with heating in it.
And so the man had it 100% better than we women had it.
And, nevertheless, less men survived than women, which is quite amazing, really.
I think we are stronger.
Physically we are stronger.
And, you know, we go-- I mean, not I at the time-- but we go through childbirth and we bring up families and we sacrifice more.
I think men live a more protected life in general.
And it must be, because they definitely had it much better.
So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we went into the different blocks.
There were quite a lot.
It was not as-- by no means as big as Birkenau was.
There were, perhaps, 20 buildings.
Quite small, actually.
But, I mean, big buildings.
So there were quite a few.
But by no means as big as Birkenau was.
Were thousands of thousands of women there.
And we went around, and in some buildings were empty and some building were people.
And we looked and ask everybody.
And then suddenly I came in a bunk somebody who looked a little bit familiar.
And I went to him and he got up and looked at me.
Was very, very thin man with a little head, with big, brown eyes.
And I said, you are Mr.
Frank.
So, he said, yeah, yeah, yeah.
And you?
I said, I'm Eva Geiringer.
He said, ah!
And, you know.
And I said, have you seen my father and my brother?
He said, yes, yes.
I've seen them.
But they went on the forced marches.
They went several weeks ago.
They were taken away.
And he asked me, of course, have you seen Anne and Margot and my wife.
And I said, no.
Never saw anybody.
So I said, my mother is here.
And we talked a bit.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we left him again.
And I looked around a bit more.
And then I came-- another familiar figure and it was this Mr.
Hirsch who had given my father and brother some of his shirts in Westerbork.
He was there as well and survived.
Otherwise, we didn't know anybody.
And-- I mean, I.
And then I tried to find the French girl and the Polish woman.
And the French girl said she's not going back to our hut.
She's going to stay here.
There was everything going on there, you know?
The Russians and she was already looking for-- trying to look nice.
And she hadn't been very long in the camp.
What was her name?
I've forgotten now.
But I might have mentioned in the book.
I don't know if I know her name now.
But she was a young, nice, French girl.
And the Polish woman said she will go back with me there the first.
And then she must have met somebody who she knew and she said as well she's staying.
I mean, they didn't have to go back.
But I, of course, had my mother there.
I had to go back.
So--
No.
She was an older woman and she was not Jewish.
I don't know.
I've forgotten now if she was a political prisoner or-- streetwalkers were as well.
There are all kind of different people.
But I don't think she was because, as I say, she was an older person.
So I decided it was time I go back because, you know, it was quite a walk and just getting dark.
Was mid winter.
And so I set off.
And it got very dark and I didn't know anymore which way to go.
I lost completely direction, everything.
And suddenly I heard terrible shooting and right on top of me I saw bullets flying.
And I saw shadows and fighting was going on right where I was walking.
And I hid behind bushes and, you know, waited till all this were finished.
And I walked on again.
But it took me many, many hours, and how I found it back, again, I don't know.
I had no way of knowing the direction or light or nothing.
And I don't know.
I think it was a bit moon but, even so, there was nothing to guide me.
But, anyway, somehow I got back to my mother.
And I told her that I met Dr.
Frank and that he had seen my father and Heinz but they had left the camp.
But at least we knew they were still alive not too long ago.
So we decided, well, there's no point staying there.
And we'll go back the next day.
So we took whatever we had taken, our blankets and my boots.
I'd taken lovely boots.
And warm jackets.
We had good clothes then, whatever.
But we couldn't carry so much, of course, because we were weak.
But whatever we could carry, we took.
And we marched back to the men's camp.
And there the Russians didn't really organize anything yet.
They were still busy getting themselves straight.
And as I say, the fighting was going on.
But there was definitely a center there.
And they tried to-- the first things they did send was to cut the bunk beds down to single beds so because they realized that it was difficult for people to go up to the top bunk.
So they organized people to come and do that.
And they said you can go and stay wherever you-- you know, move in wherever.
And we didn't want to live with the men.
So on top of a building there was a little room where we said we are going to live there for the time being.
And we put all our things there and we went out again to find things.
And one day I found a big liver sausage in the street.
And took that to our home and I ate the whole liver sausage.
And you can imagine what pain I had and what cramps.
I was again in agony and then I decided that I just must not eat so much because it's going to kill my inside.
And one day a lot of our things were stolen from our little room.
You know, things we had-- I mean, it was all stolen goods.
It was nothing that belonged to us, but things which we had planned to keep was gone again.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we tried to make do with what we had.
And we-- I don't know.
We did not really do anything.
We just walked around.
Again, just trying to eat and to speak a bit with the people.
And one day a Russian came and asked he need somebody to window clean.
And this person will get special food.
So my mother volunteered.
She would clean the window in this office, from his office.
And so she said I have nothing to clean it, so he said, newspaper is the best.
So he gave her a bucket and newspaper and he said, you wash the windows with newspaper.
And she did that and she did get some-- I don't know.
Some more cabbage soup or something.
And then-- and other days they wanted people for translating, for German and Russian.
And my mother helped with German.
I mean, she couldn't translate Russia, but there was somebody else helping.
So my mother tried to make herself useful.
So she was still very, very weak.
I don't know how she did it.
And she had no hair and a very knobbly head.
And she had to have a blue dress with a high collar and very, very short and no stockings and high, high black boots.
And, you know, she looked quite terrible, really.
You know, I can see her still in front of me.
I don't remember what I had.
I remember I had black boots and riding breeches, but what I had on top, I can't remember.
But she had nothing.
No coat or anything.
Don't know why we didn't do-- take that.
But we had lots of blankets.
And one day she took an old blanket and she was sewing-- said I'll sew you a rucksack so that we could carry things around in it.
And I still got this rucksack.
I'll show that to you.
It's quite interesting, actually.
And then one day the Russian said, look, it's too dangerous to stay here because we are not-- we are losing position and we are going to move you more east.
So they got us on lorries and took us to railway station.
And we went to Katowice.
And there we-- they launched us in empty building, and on the floor.
But then we were already registered.
They registered us.
I've got a little document for that.
And they said, well, when the war will end, you'll go back wherever.
But at the moment you have to stay here.
That would be February, 1945.
And the war was still going on, of course.
And so from Katowice they moved us all the time more east.
And--
Again on the train in-- same as we traveled with the Germans, only it wasn't locked.
And they put a partition so that there were two-- a top partition and a low partition.
And they did give us some-- a little stove in the middle.
And one could cook sometimes, sometimes some-- if you had something.
And they don't-- it was opened all the time.
And it was different.
And, of course, then we realized it was quite a different-- we were quite cheerful.
And each time we traveled through the villages and the train stopped, first the Poles and later the Russian farmer's women came onto the trucks to bargain with us if we had something.
They were selling eggs or chicken or-- and they wanted goods.
Textiles, scarves, blankets, clothes.
And we did have bits and pieces.
And we did swap it for all kind of food again.
Always food.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] and so we traveled to-- many, many days.
Sometimes we were weeks on the trucks.
And when we stopped they told us we can get out and we didn't like to go on the bucket in the train, so we went always outside at the railway side.
And one day [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother was outside.
I say-- we were all outside.
And suddenly the train slowly started to move.
And we all-- it was high, you know?
And we all tried to clamber in and something.
And my mother didn't so quickly and the train started to speed up.
And we all put our hands on, tried to get her up, but she couldn't.
And so the train left and she was left behind.
And I was terribly upset, of course.
And we didn't know where we were going.
And-- but, I mean, there was nothing you could do about it.
So the next place was a channel, which it was Romania.
And there again we went into that town and we left in an old school building or something.
And we stayed there a long time.
And we went out into the town.
And we went into Jewish houses when there was a mezuzah, because the Romanians, a lot of them have not been transported.
A lot of Romanian Jews survived.
And we knocked on the door and we told who we are.
We said we have no money.
We have nothing.
And they asked us in for-- gave us food and were very good and wanted to know, of course, what had happened.
And even once we went to a cinema and said we have no money and we are from concentration camp.
Can we go to film?
We started to be more worldly by then.
And-- but a lot of the inmates-- I mean, we didn't have good treatment, you know?
We were not treated as delicately.
We had to-- once, for instance, the Russian in the middle of the night woke us up and called out, we need people to peel potatoes.
We have to cook a soup because a army transport is coming and we have to feed the soldiers in the night in order that they can go on.
And a lot of people said, oh, but we are weak.
We are from the concentration camp.
We can't work in the night.
We need our sleep.
And, you know, we're-- But I was always joining in with them.
And when they're finished cooking, they got out their musical instrument, a balalaika, which is sort of a guitar but more stringy.
And they sang and danced.
And, you know, the Russian dances they did.
And they tried to teach us.
And, you know, they were very basic and very kind and jolly.
And, I mean, some of them had photos of their family which they showed us.
And these are all dead, you know?
All hanged dead.
And then some were in German camps, in prison camp, and had escaped.
Some did speak German and they told us-- they had with the photos of their family always they had a photo of Stalin.
And they said Stalin, kiss.
Then they told us-- they said their parents, they had nothing.
Nothing to eat.
They were worse than dogs treated.
And when Stalin came they got a little land and they could grow their own food.
And it was paradise compared to how it was under the tsars.
So that's how I got a bit influenced, because I really thought that Stalin was wonderful.
And-- but all of them, all of the soldiers, all of them told us the same stories.
So, obviously, the stories which came out later of the killings of Stalin wasn't known to the general population.
So the same, like the Germans sometimes say, they didn't know what had happened in the concentration camps, the Russians obviously didn't know about how Stalin mistreated a lot of their population.
But I loved them, I must say.
I have nothing bad to say about-- no bad instant.
Well, there was a bad incident, but I didn't consider it as a bad incident.
Yes.
Oh, right.
